project_description.text: San Diego Gas & Electric Company, with seven other participants, proposes to build the Sundesert Nuclear Plant—two pressurized-water reactors of about 974 MWe each—on a 2,859-hectare site on the Palo Verde Mesa in Riverside County, California, roughly 9 km west of the Colorado River and 26 km southwest of Blythe. Cooling water (up to about 2 m³/sec) would come from the Palo Verde Outfall Drain, with mechanical-draft cooling towers and a lined evaporation basin so that no thermal or chemical effluents reach surface waters. The project also includes about 618 km of new 500-kV and 230-kV transmission line rights-of-way connecting the plant to substations near San Diego and Riverside, plus access roads, a rail spur from Ripley, and communication sites. Unit 1 is scheduled to begin commercial operation in 1984 and Unit 2 in 1986, with an expected operating life of 30–35 years.

affected_community.text: The plant site is in a sparsely populated desert area near the small communities of Palo Verde and Ripley (each about 200–300 residents), with Blythe (the main service center) about 26 km to the northeast; only about 16 permanent residents lived within 3–5 km in 1970. A peak construction workforce of roughly 2,800 and about 200 permanent operations workers would strain housing, schools, hospitals, and water and sewer services in the Palo Verde Valley. The transmission corridors cross or pass near many additional communities—including Indio, Coachella, Palm Springs, Banning, Beaumont, Temecula, Escondido, El Cajon, Calexico, Jacumba, and Campo—as well as the Morongo, Pechanga, and Pala Indian Reservations and agricultural users in the Imperial and Coachella Valleys.

alternatives_overview.text: The NRC staff evaluated alternatives to the project including purchased power, postponing retirement or upgrading of existing units, and other generating sources such as coal, oil, gas, geothermal, hydroelectric, solar, wind, biomass, fuel cells, and fusion, concluding that only a coal-fired plant was a reasonable alternative—and that coal would be 4–12 times more harmful to human health than the nuclear option. Fourteen candidate site areas were screened, with Palo Verde North, Blythe, Milpitas Wash, Parker Valley, and Camp Pendleton receiving closer review. Cooling-system options compared included multicell and single-cell mechanical-draft, wet-dry, dry, spray-canal, cooling-pond, once-through, and natural-draft towers, with multicell mechanical-draft selected. For transmission, the staff weighed northern versus southern 500-kV corridors, multiple BLM alternative segments (I-1 through I-9 and II-1 through II-8), undergrounding, helicopter construction in sensitive areas, and tower design choices, ultimately preferring several BLM alternatives over portions of the applicant's proposed route.

environmental_impact.text: Plant construction would disturb about 300 hectares on site and 101 hectares offsite, with short-term turbidity, siltation, and possible mercury resuspension where intake structures are built in the Palo Verde Outfall Drain, Oxbow Lake, and the Colorado River. Cooling-tower salt drift could raise soil salinity enough to kill creosote bush and bursage on up to 2,400–4,000 hectares around the plant, and water withdrawals could expose bank habitat used by the endangered Yuma clapper rail. The 618 km of transmission corridors would clear hundreds of hectares and cause high visual, recreational, biological, and archaeological impacts across desert, mountain, and agricultural landscapes—including the Chuckwalla and Orocopia Mountains, Algodones Dunes, Anza-Borrego State Park, and potential National Register sites along historic stage routes—with desert vegetation recovery potentially exceeding 100 years. Routine radiological releases and accident risks would remain well below regulatory limits and small compared with natural background, while occupational exposure is estimated at about 1,000 person-rem per year for the two units.

public_response.text: Community surveys along the corridors found that visual intrusion was the most widely shared concern, followed by worries about radio and television interference (especially among elderly residents in remote desert communities), interference with crop dusting and irrigation, and effects on future community growth. The Morongo, Pechanga, and Pala Indian Reservations raised concerns about land-use fragmentation, archaeological disturbance, and health and safety, and backcountry users and agencies strongly opposed routings through Anza-Borrego State Park and other outstanding recreational areas. To address these concerns, the applicant established a Desert Valley Impact Committee and a Riverside Task Force involving nearly 100 local leaders, sponsored an Imperial Valley Committee to suggest agricultural-area reroutes, and committed to a contingency plan with a designated complaint officer; the California Energy Commission, State Historic Preservation Officer, Resources Agency, and Forest Service also submitted formal concerns about siting, cultural resources, and forest crossings.

alternatives.note: Alternatives chapter not detected structurally — skipped per v2 plan (no word-regex fallback).

themes.justification: The project centers on nuclear power generation (Energy Extraction and Production) and 618 km of new high-voltage transmission lines (Energy Distribution and Consumption; Infrastructure Impacts on Landscapes), while the environmental impact field highlights threats to the endangered Yuma clapper rail, salt drift killing desert vegetation across thousands of hectares, and long-term habitat disruption across sensitive desert and mountain landscapes (Habitat Conservation and Biodiversity; Human-Wildlife Interactions).
